skill.name: backend-architect
skill.description: Senior backend architecture guidance for designing, reviewing, testing, and refactoring services with hexagonal architecture, DDD, SOLID, and clean code practices.
skill.summary: Review and improve backend architecture with DDD, SOLID, testing, and refactoring guidance.
skill.author: Bikach

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk weak-cryptography and Ruby backtick findings, but reviewed examples show markdown diagrams, prose, paths, and architecture terminology rather than executable cryptographic code or shell execution. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, network behavior, credential harvesting, or malicious intent was found. The remaining risk is medium because the skill allows Bash and recommends command-based review workflows.

medium_findings.title: Bash Tooling Can Execute Project Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash as an allowed tool and recommends git diff, grep, and project linters or formatters. This is legitimate for backend code review, but it gives the assistant command execution capability in a user workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct evidence in SKILL.md shows Bash is allowed and command-oriented workflows are recommended. No malicious commands were found, so confidence is high for elevated capability but not for abuse.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Cryptography Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak-cryptography matches occur in DDD, SOLID, architecture terms, markdown diagrams, and checklist prose. I found no evidence of weak cryptographic APIs or recommendations to use weak algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed locations contain architecture headings, file paths, and design-pattern prose rather than cryptographic implementation. The repeated scanner hits align with terminology, not security-sensitive code.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external-command scanner matched markdown code fences and inline architecture examples. These sections document folder structures and refactoring examples, not Ruby shell backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown fences containing diagrams or pseudo-structure. They do not contain Ruby code, command substitution, or user-controlled command execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tooling Can Execute Project Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill declares Bash as an allowed tool and recommends git diff, grep, and project linters or formatters. This is legitimate for backend code review, but it gives the assistant command execution capability in a user workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct evidence in SKILL.md shows Bash is allowed and command-oriented workflows are recommended. No malicious commands were found, so confidence is high for elevated capability but not for abuse.

content.user_title: Review Backend Architecture with Senior Guidance
content.value_statement: Backend services can drift into tight coupling, unclear responsibilities, and fragile tests. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through architecture review, refactoring, and implementation decisions.
actual_capabilities: Reviews backend code changes for architecture, layering, and dependency direction.
Applies hexagonal architecture, DDD, and SOLID checklists to feature design.
Identifies common code smells such as god classes, long methods, and primitive obsession.
Suggests targeted refactoring steps with priority, impact, and recommended fixes.
Uses testing, performance, and clean code checklists to improve implementation quality.
Provides reference structures for ports, adapters, use cases, and bounded contexts.
limitations: It is focused on backend architecture, not frontend design or infrastructure operations.
Its examples are conceptual and may need adaptation for each framework or language.
Bash-based review steps should be scoped to trusted repository commands.
It does not replace project-specific product, security, or compliance review.

use_cases.title: Design a New Backend Feature
use_cases.description: Plan bounded contexts, ports, adapters, use cases, and test strategy before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Review a Pull Request
use_cases.description: Check recent changes for SOLID violations, layer leaks, code smells, and missing tests.

use_cases.title: Refactor a Legacy Service
use_cases.description: Break down large services, move logic into domain models, and introduce clearer abstractions.

prompt_templates.title: Review My Backend Change
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small change
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the current backend changes for architecture, SOLID principles, code smells, and missing tests. Prioritize issues as P0, P1, or P2.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Feature Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a backend structure for this feature using hexagonal architecture. Identify domain models, use cases, ports, adapters, and test coverage.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Refactor
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this service for god class, feature envy, primitive obsession, long method, and shotgun surgery risks. Propose a staged refactoring plan.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Architecture Boundaries
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit the module boundaries and dependency direction across these backend contexts. Find layer violations, coupling risks, and missing abstractions.

output_examples.input: Review a pull request that adds a user creation use case.
output: P1: The use case depends on a concrete repository instead of a port.
P1: Password hashing should stay behind a domain port.
P2: Add integration tests for the repository adapter and unit tests for domain validation.

output_examples.input: Design the backend layout for a payment feature.
output_examples.output: The result should separate payment domain models from infrastructure adapters, define payment gateway ports, and add use case tests for success and failure paths.

output_examples.input: Find refactoring opportunities in a large service class.
output: Extract separate use cases for commands that change state.
Move validation rules into value objects where they protect domain invariants.
Introduce ports before replacing infrastructure dependencies.

best_practices: Start with the business use case, then define domain models, ports, and adapters.
Ask for prioritized findings with file locations, impact, and recommended fixes.
Run only necessary repository commands and review results before applying changes.
anti_patterns: Using the skill as a general shell automation tool instead of an architecture reviewer.
Requesting broad rewrites before identifying the smallest useful refactoring step.
Applying DDD or hexagonal architecture mechanically when the service does not need it.

faq.question: What kind of projects fit this skill?
faq.answer: It fits backend services that need architecture review, refactoring, or feature design guidance.

faq.question: Can it write production code?
faq.answer: It can guide implementation and suggest changes, but users should review all generated code.

faq.question: Does it require a specific language?
faq.answer: No. The guidance is pattern based and can be adapted to common backend languages.

faq.question: Why does it need Bash?
faq.answer: It uses Bash-capable workflows for commands such as git diff, grep, linters, and formatters.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium warning because command execution is available through Bash.

faq.question: What should users provide?
faq.answer: Provide the feature goal, relevant files, recent changes, and any architecture constraints.
